lenses.guidance: The 'be fruitful and multiply' refrain (v 7) deliberately echoes Gen 1:28 — Israel's multiplication is creation-mandate language, framing Pharaoh's policy (v 10) as anti-creation. The narrative's repetition is the structure of the conflict.

Pharaoh's three escalating policies — hard labour (v 11), midwife conscription (v 16), Nile decree (v 22) — all fail to suppress covenant multiplication. The redemptive arc begins with empire's strategic exhaustion against promise.

Shiphrah and Puah's civil disobedience (vv 17-21) is named approvingly across Scripture — the pattern threads to Daniel 3, Acts 4:19 and 5:29 ('we must obey God rather than men'). The first righteous act in Exodus is a refusal.
Pharaoh is unnamed; Shiphrah and Puah are named (v 15). God's moral memory inverts imperial visibility. Today, trust that quiet faithfulness leaves a name where loud power leaves only a title.
